splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing requirements for actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing of assumption selection directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about the limits of judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the clock stops running and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that no judicially-created fugitive tolling doctrine may extend the term of supervised release beyond the time specified in the judgment, as there is no explicit statutory authorization for such tolling.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the stricter gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars raising any previously presented claim — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners face an additional bar beyond § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements and are not subject to § 2244(b)(1)'s separate categorical bar.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution affects whether candidates can challenge rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices before an election takes place, or whether such disputes must be litigated post-election.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate for federal office suffers a cognizable injury sufficient for Article III standing when a state law changes the rules under which ballots are counted, such as extending the receipt deadline for mail-in ballots.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized interest in election rules does not constitute a concrete, particularized injury sufficient for Article III standing to challenge such laws pre-election.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments?
splits.description: The circuits have taken different approaches to the standards for establishing liability when business operators discriminate against or unfairly treat customers. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach, indicating a circuit split on the relevant legal questions affecting hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: The Second Circuit applied its own standards for evaluating discrimination claims in hospitality/business establishment contexts, which the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for evaluating discrimination or unfair treatment claims against business establishments, creating a split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) in interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while the Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has gained significance after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference, casting further doubt on deference in criminal contexts.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference may override the rule of lenity, allowing deference to anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for many drug offenders.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is disqualifying from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as effectively meaning 'or.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which expands the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, is a valid and authoritative interpretation of the Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary adding inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) to the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions can be sentenced as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is valid and authoritative
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of a genuinely ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight, so inchoate offenses qualify as career-offender predicates.

positions.label: Commentary is invalid — text is unambiguous
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so the commentary impermissibly expands the definition and deserves no deference under Kisor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controls a substance not listed on the federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the career-offender and other sentencing guidelines require the substance underlying a state conviction to be federally controlled. Some circuits hold the guidelines broadly cover any state drug offense, while others require the substance to also be federally scheduled. The split affects guideline calculations for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines broadly includes state offenses regardless of whether the particular substance is controlled under federal law, as there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Federal scheduling required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can be considered as a factor supporting 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences — when evaluating compassionate release motions. Some circuits hold such changes can never contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others hold they may be considered alone or in combination with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered as part of, or in combination with other factors supporting, 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in good faith under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that have held it belongs to the debtor under the statutory scheme.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a)'s definition of 'property of the estate,' means post-petition appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure establishes that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors, consistent with the statute's object, policies, and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the civil appeal deadline) or governed by the criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but acknowledged a lopsided circuit split on the question.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline (typically 30 or 60 days) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, so the criminal appeal deadline (14 days) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking and resentencing, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit, under its precedent in United States v. Lewis, allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release. Dissenting judges argue this places the Sixth Circuit at the extreme of a circuit split, as other circuits limit revocation sentences to sanctioning the breach of the court's trust rather than punishing the underlying violative conduct.

positions.label: Punishment for violations is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for their violations of supervised release conditions when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on the defendant's breach of the court's trust in granting supervised release, not as punishment for the underlying conduct that violated the conditions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine. This implicates broader disagreement among circuits about the scope of the collateral order doctrine for privacy-related sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment and involves a value of sufficiently high order to warrant immediate review under the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal does not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because privacy interests can be adequately vindicated on appeal from final judgment or through other means.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any... broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims arising from a broker's selection of a motor carrier. This affects liability standards for the entire freight brokerage industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation, falling within the FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence — claims that might also be raised under § 2255 — as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for a sentence reduction under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release can serve as a vehicle for addressing alleged legal errors in a conviction or sentence that would ordinarily be raised in a habeas petition. Some circuits permit courts to consider such claims as one factor in the compassionate release analysis, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used to circumvent the requirements of § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider potential legal errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for conviction errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used to address claims of legal error that properly belong in a § 2255 motion, and such claims do not constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons.
